professor.name: Dr. Diaz
professor.text: More and more companies have recently started using AI systems to scan resumes and rank job applicants before any human sees them. Supporters argue that this saves recruiters enormous time and can reduce individual bias, while critics worry that the software quietly inherits the very prejudices hidden in past hiring data. Should companies be allowed to let AI make the first cut in hiring? Why or why not?

students.name: Andrew
students.text: I believe letting AI handle the first round is reasonable, as long as people review the results afterward. A recruiter reading four thousand applications will get tired and inconsistent, whereas software applies the same standard to everyone. The key is auditing what the system actually rewards.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: In my opinion, handing that first decision to an algorithm is risky. These systems learn from whoever a company hired before, so they quietly repeat old patterns and reject people who look different on paper. A human recruiter can at least explain a choice.

professor.name: Dr. Achebe
professor.text: Many of the world's most famous museums hold sculptures, manuscripts, and other treasures that were removed from distant countries during colonial times. Some historians insist these objects should be returned to their places of origin as a matter of justice, while others argue that great museums preserve and share such works with a far wider global audience. Should these artifacts be sent back to their countries of origin?

students.name: Claire
students.text: Returning these treasures is the only fair option. They were taken without real consent during a period when their communities had no power to refuse, and holding onto them now extends that old injustice. A culture deserves to display its own history at home.

students.name: Paul
students.text: Sending everything back sounds noble but ignores some practical realities. Major museums protect fragile objects with resources that many regions cannot yet match, and they let millions of visitors encounter cultures they would never otherwise see. Shared access can matter as much as ownership does.
